articles.title: Parents of US citizen missing in Japan describe 'total nightmare’
articles.description: STORY: :: Nancy Higginbotham :: Kyoto, Japan / June 4, 2026 :: The parents of Weston Higginbotham, a missing U.S. student in Japan, describe the situation as a 'total nightmare' :: Nancy Higginbotham, Mother of Weston Higginbotham :: Keith Higginbotham, Father of Weston Higginbotham NANCY: “It's just been a total nightmare. And like (my husband) Keith said, this is day five and a half, and it's like hard to even cry now because you get to a state where you're just purely numb." KEITH: “When you've told this story so many times...” NANCY: “So numb.” KEITH: “Sadly, it's easier to say these words when you say it over and over and over :: Nancy Higginbotham :: Japanese police said they found CCTV footage showing Weston exiting Yamashina station the night he disappeared “And then he got on the train, and we started texting him like, ‘hey, where are you going? Like, what are you doing?’ Because we see him on Life360. And you know, he doesn't respond. And I'm like, okay, he just, you know, he just needs space. And we just keep kind of pinging him like, hey, like, Weston, what are you doing? Like, where are you? And then his phone goes dark, like completely dark, location turned off. And that is the last time that we were able to track him.” :: Nancy Higginbotham :: The 20-year-old student has been missing since May 29 The Higginbothams later visited a local police station, where they filed a report. They had hoped that Weston, an avid hiker and outdoorsman, would eventually return to the hotel the next day. He never did. It was by Monday (June 1) that the police said they had found CCTV footage showing Weston exiting Yamashina station, about a five-minute ride from Kyoto’s main station, at around 8:30 p.m. from the night he went missing. As the investigation dragged on, the Higginbothams began posting across social media, handing out pamphlets and posters, and even searching the forested area around Yamashina station themselves before the Japanese police deployed a full search team of 50 officers, K9s and helicopters from Wednesday (June 3). While Nancy said she is quite grateful for the efforts of the Japanese police and the U.S. embassy, she also commended the “incredible” assistance she and her family have received from Japanese citizens who have volunteered to help with everything from interpretation, to designing pamphlets, and searching across Yamashina area on motorcycles. The Higginbothams traveled from Alabama to Japan to celebrate their younger son’s high school graduation. The family had been traveling together since Monday (May 25), according to mother Nancy Higginbotham’s social media posts.

articles.title: 'Bringing in Iraola is the best option - Murphy
articles.description: Former Liverpool midfielder Danny Murphy feels the Reds will be in a much better position next season with Andoni Iraola than they were under Arne Slot. "I'm quite excited because after watching Bournemouth play, there's no reason to think he's not going to try to play the same way - in terms of being courageous and on the front foot, and trying to press teams with high energy and play attacking football," Murphy said. Managers have often struggled to make the step up in the Premier League in the past, the most recent example when Thomas Frank joined Tottenham from Brentford.
